oneSentenceAnswer: Creatine is a cheap, exceptionally well-studied supplement that helps muscles refuel energy for short, hard efforts — the monohydrate form at 3–5 g a day reliably builds strength and power, and it's safe for healthy people.
fiveMinutePath: Learn what creatine does in one sentence.
See the simple energy mechanism (it refuels ATP).
Get the dose that works: 3–5 g/day of monohydrate.
Clear up the big myths: water weight, loading, kidneys.
Know what's solid vs still-emerging (e.g. brain effects).
whyItMatters: It's one of the few supplements with decades of strong evidence — so knowing the facts saves you money and skips the marketing for fancier, pricier forms that don't work better.
Most fears about it (kidney damage, it's 'just water') are misconceptions the research has directly addressed — being clear-eyed lets you decide on evidence, not gym folklore.

keyIdeas.title: Monohydrate, 3–5 g a day
keyIdeas.body: Creatine **monohydrate** is the most-studied and most effective form — citrate, HCl, 'ethyl ester', and buffered versions don't beat it. Once your muscles are topped up, 3–5 g a day keeps them there (larger athletes may need a bit more).

keyIdeas.title: It refuels your energy battery
keyIdeas.body: Creatine is stored in muscle as **phosphocreatine**, which rapidly regenerates ATP — the cell's energy currency — during the first ~0–30 seconds of intense effort. More phosphocreatine means more reps, sprints, and power.

keyIdeas.title: Loading is optional
keyIdeas.body: A 'loading' phase (~20 g/day for 5–7 days) fills your stores fast. But it's not required: 3–5 g/day reaches the same level in about four weeks. Skipping the load just means the early benefits arrive a little later.

keyIdeas.title: Not 'just water weight'
keyIdeas.body: The first couple of pounds are mostly water drawn into the muscle. But over 5–12 weeks of training, studies show real gains in lean muscle and strength — the water claim is misleading, not the whole story.

keyIdeas.title: Safe for healthy people
keyIdeas.body: At recommended doses — even up to 30 g/day for 5 years in studies — creatine is safe and doesn't harm kidneys in healthy people. If you already have kidney disease, check with a clinician first.

keyIdeas.title: Brain effects: promising, not settled
keyIdeas.body: Beyond muscle, creatine may help thinking under sleep deprivation and in older adults, and can raise brain creatine 5–15%. The research uses hedged language here — treat it as emerging, not proven like the strength benefits.

diagrams.alt: Energy cycle: creatine in food or a supplement is stored in muscle as phosphocreatine; during a hard effort, phosphocreatine donates a phosphate to ADP to rapidly regenerate ATP, the muscle's energy, powering short bursts; ATP is used and becomes ADP again, repeating.

timeline.title: You start
timeline.body: Pick creatine monohydrate. Decide: load fast, or go slow and steady.

timeline.title: Optional loading
timeline.body: ~20 g/day (split into 4 doses) saturates muscle stores in about a week.

timeline.title: No-load alternative
timeline.body: Just 3–5 g/day reaches the same stores in about four weeks — no loading needed.

timeline.title: Maintain
timeline.body: 3–5 g/day keeps stores topped up. Strength and lean-mass gains build over weeks of training.

examples.title: Where it helps most
examples.body: Lifting, sprinting, jumping, repeated hard intervals — short, intense bursts. It does little for steady endurance like long-distance running.

analogies.setup: Think of ATP as your phone battery and phosphocreatine as a small power bank clipped to it.
analogies.takeaway: Creatine makes the power bank bigger, so you can recharge faster between hard, short efforts.

misconceptions.truth: The first pound or two is water pulled into the muscle, but training studies show real lean-mass and strength gains over 5–12 weeks.

misconceptions.myth: You must do a loading phase.
misconceptions.truth: Loading is optional. 3–5 g/day reaches the same stores in about four weeks.
No evidence of kidney harm in healthy people at recommended doses, even long-term. Those with kidney disease should ask a clinician.

glossary.definition: A compound your body makes and gets from meat/fish; it helps muscles produce quick energy.
The cheapest, best-studied, most effective form — the one to buy.
Creatine stored in muscle with a phosphate attached; it rapidly refuels ATP.
Adenosine triphosphate — the molecule cells spend for energy. Refueling it faster means more power.
A short period (~5–7 days) of higher doses to fill muscle stores quickly. Optional.

practicalTakeaways: Buy plain creatine monohydrate — skip the pricier 'advanced' forms.
Take 3–5 g a day, any time; consistency matters more than timing.
Skip loading if you don't mind waiting ~4 weeks for full effect.
If you have kidney disease, talk to a clinician before starting.

sourceMap.title: ISSN position stand: safety and efficacy of creatine supplementation in exercise, sport, and medicine
sourceMap.publisher: J. Int. Society of Sports Nutrition
sourceMap.summary: Definitive consensus: monohydrate is the most effective form; 3–5 g/day maintains stores; safe long-term; no renal harm in healthy people.

sourceMap.title: Common questions and misconceptions about creatine supplementation
sourceMap.publisher: J. Int. Society of Sports Nutrition
sourceMap.summary: Rebuts the water-weight, kidney, and mandatory-loading myths; monohydrate is optimal; 3–5 g/day for ≥4 weeks without loading.

sourceMap.title: Creatine supplementation is safe, beneficial throughout the lifespan, and should not be restricted
sourceMap.summary: 2025 consensus: safety and efficacy across all life stages.

sourceMap.title: Creatine for Exercise and Sports Performance, with Recovery Considerations for Healthy Populations
sourceMap.summary: Ergogenic for short, high-intensity bouts; limited endurance benefit; explains the phosphocreatine→ATP mechanism.

sourceMap.title: Creatine Supplementation for Muscle Growth: A Scoping Review of RCTs 2012–2021
sourceMap.summary: RCT review: effective for strength, mass, and performance in trained healthy young people.

sourceMap.title: Dietary Supplements for Exercise and Athletic Performance (Health Professional Fact Sheet)
sourceMap.summary: US government fact sheet: evidence supports creatine for repeated short bursts of high-intensity exercise.

sourceMap.title: Creatine — Research Breakdown
sourceMap.summary: Independent evidence synthesis: improves high-intensity performance in ~70% of studies; safe at 3–5 g/day.

uncertaintyNotes: Without a loading phase, primary sources (ISSN 2017; JISSN 2021) cite about 4 weeks of 3–5 g/day to reach full muscle stores — a little longer than some popular '3-week' claims.
Cognitive/brain benefits (e.g. under sleep deprivation, in older adults) are promising but described in hedged language by the sources — less settled than the muscle and strength effects.
These are general-population findings, not medical advice. People with kidney disease or who are pregnant should consult a clinician.
